images.caption: Figure 1:Lip Forcing.A streaming model for real-time lip synchronization that produces photorealistic, accurately lip-synced video at up to31 FPSwith low latency and memory.Right:both student scales lie on the throughput–FVD Pareto frontier, ahead of prior diffusion lip-sync methods.

Figure 2:Trajectory analysis of the 14B teacher.Bands are±1\pm 1SE.(a) CFG fidelity–sync tradeoff:CFG (s=4.5s{=}4.5, red) improves Sync-C but worsens reference fidelity (LPIPS), while no-CFG (s=1.0s{=}1.0, navy) shows the opposite trend.(b) Euler-step2×22{\times}2factorialover schedules(s0,s1)(s_{0},s_{1}), plotted against the second-step landingj1j_{1}: mixed schedules recover most of the sync gap of the CFG-guided ceiling at landings near step 30.
Full 4-metric versions in App.C.2.

Figure 3:Why few-step distillation needs trajectory-level care.Two HDTF[52]samples, each showing the 1-step prediction from pure noise, 50-step ODE final output, and ground truth, respectively.
Even a one-step prediction preserves coarse facial structure and approximate mouth timing, but it loses the fine articulation and audio-visual synchronization recovered by the full 50-step teacher.
Lip Forcing compresses this gap with a two-step student via the trajectory analysis of Sec.4.2.
Figure 4:Fixed-CFG endpoints vs. diagnostic operating point(green diamond, at ODE stepj=30j=30).n=10n{=}10,±1\pm 1SE. SSIM and 4-metric in App.C.2.

Figure 5:Architecture of Lip Forcing.The causal student denoises Gaussian noise with lip-sync conditions, producing a chunk-wise causal rollout via the two-step schedule (Sec.4.4).
The clean predictionx^0\hat{x}_{0}is supervised by the DMD[48,47]gradient (Eq.4) between a frozen 14B teacher and a trainable fake-score critic, with the teacher’s CFG gated by the windowed schedulesSWs_{\mathrm{SW}}of Eq.6.
The samex^0\hat{x}_{0}is decoded by the frozen Tiny AutoEncoder (TAE)[2]and scored by frozen SyncNet[7]against the conditioning audio to form the reward weightexp⁡(β​R)\exp(\beta R)on the generator gradient (Eq.7).

Figure 6:Qualitative comparison on HDTF.Each row shows the same source frame rendered by our method, six lip-sync baselines, and the ground truth (GT) at the moment of articulating the bracketed English phoneme. Best viewed zoomed in and in color.

Figure 7:The CFG fidelity–sync tradeoff (full 4-metric).Per-step mean acrossn=10n{=}10samples; shaded bands are±1\pm 1standard error. Red: CFG-guided teacher (s=4.5s{=}4.5); navy: no-CFG teacher (s=1.0s{=}1.0). SSIM (mouth) tracks LPIPS, and Sync-D mirrors Sync-C: the same separation between the two trajectories observed in the main figure is reproduced on these additional metrics.

Figure 8:Euler-step2×22\times 2factorial (full 4-metric).Per-step mean acrossn=10n{=}10samples; shaded bands are±1\pm 1standard error. Each trace is one cell of(s0,s1)(s_{0},s_{1}). The reference-axis pattern (cells sharings0s_{0}converge by mid-trajectory) holds on SSIM as well as LPIPS; the sync-axis pattern (single-CFG cells close most of the gap to CFG→\toCFG around step 30, then diverge outside the mid-trajectory window) holds on Sync-D as well as Sync-C.

Figure 9:Fixed-CFG endpoints vs. schedule operating point (full 4-panel).Step-49 endpoints of fixed-CFG sweeps ats∈{1.0,3.0,4.5,6.0}s\in\{1.0,3.0,4.5,6.0\}as open circles; the no-CFG→\toCFG Euler-step operating point atj=30j{=}30as a filled green diamond. Both axes carry±1\pm 1SE error bars onn=10n{=}10samples. Axes are oriented so up-right is favorable (LPIPS, Sync-D inverted). The Sync-D panels (the right column) tell the same story as the Sync-C panels reproduced in the main paper.

Figure 10:CFG fidelity–sync tradeoff, audio-only drop mode.Audio-only counterpart of main Fig.2(a) (full 4-metric counterpart in Fig.7). Per-step means on the mouth region acrossn=10n{=}10samples; shaded bands are±1\pm 1standard error. Red:s=4.5s{=}4.5with audio-only drop. Navy:s=1.0s{=}1.0(drop mode irrelevant when guidance scale is 1.0). The same direction of separation holds across all four metrics under audio-only drop.

Figure 11:Euler-step CFG factorial, audio-only drop mode.Audio-only counterpart of main Fig.2(b) (full 4-metric counterpart in Fig.8). Per-step means on the mouth region acrossn=10n{=}10samples; shaded bands are±1\pm 1standard error. Same four cells(s0,s1)(s_{0},s_{1})as the main paper:s0s_{0}drives the velocity from noise;s1s_{1}is used at the re-evaluated landing. Both axes of separation persist: cells sharings0s_{0}converge on SSIM and LPIPS by mid-trajectory; both single-CFG cells (green, purple) close most of the sync gap to the CFG→\toCFG ceiling around landings near step 30, and diverge outside this window.

Figure 12:Trajectory plateau zoom around the joint reference-sync optimum.Per-step means on the mouth region acrossn=10n{=}10samples; shaded bands are±1\pm 1standard error. Same four Euler-step cells(s0,s1)(s_{0},s_{1})as main Fig.2(b), restricted to landing stepsj1∈[15,45]j_{1}\in[15,45]. The plateau regionj1∈[25,32]j_{1}\in[25,32]is shaded gold. Within the plateau, the no-CFG→\toCFG cell (green) achieves Sync-C and Sync-D close to the CFG→\toCFG ceiling (red) while keeping mouth-LPIPS close to the no-CFG→\tono-CFG floor (navy) — the recipe’s joint optimum.

Figure 14:Throughput–FVD Pareto frontier across all baselines on HDTF.Companion to the diffusion-only chart in the main paper (Fig.1): adds the single-pass methods Wav2Lip[33], VideoReTalking[5], and MuseTalk[51]that are excluded from the main-body diffusion-only comparison. Self Forcing and the ground-truth row are still omitted; the FVD axis is inverted so the up-right corner is the best Pareto position. Vertical dotted line:2525-FPS playback rate; dashed line: Pareto frontier. Lip Forcing (14B) achieves the lowest FVD on the chart (107.88107.88), while Wav2Lip’s frontier position is throughput-only – its FVD (384.82384.82) is∼3.5×\sim 3.5\timesthat of Lip Forcing (14B), which is why the main-paper figure restricts the comparison to diffusion-based peers.

Figure 15:Long-video qualitative results on HDTFlong.Two identities, each rolled out tot=180t{=}180s and sampled every3030s, comparing ground truth, Lip Forcing, and the strongest baseline X-Dub at consistent timestamps.
Frame quality, identity, and background remain stable across the full33-minute rollout under Lip Forcing’s causal AR streaming, well beyond the8181-frame (∼\sim3.243.24s) training chunk.

Figure 16:Cross-identity qualitative results on HDTF.Two source clips are driven by audio from a different speaker (top row,Audio Source); columns mark the moments at which the highlighted English phoneme is articulated.
Each column compares Wav2Lip, VideoReTalking, Diff2Lip, X-Dub, MuseTalk, LatentSync, and Lip Forcing against the same source frame.
Lip motion in Lip Forcing follows the driving audio rather than tracking the source speaker’s original mouth shape.
Figure 17:Additional qualitative results from the Hallo3 and HDTF test sets.
Figure 18:Additional qualitative results from the TalkVid test set.
